title: Packet Analyzers
description: Practice using packet analysis tools including Wireshark and tcpdump, understanding capture filters and display filters, interpreting packet captures, and analyzing network traffic for troubleshooting.
starterCode:
# PACKET ANALYZERS EXERCISE

## Part 1: Packet Analysis Fundamentals

1. A packet _______ (also called a protocol analyzer or sniffer) captures and inspects network traffic.
2. Packet analysis operates at the _______ level, capturing individual frames as they traverse the network.
3. To capture traffic on a switched network, you may need to configure a _______ port (also called mirror port) on the switch.
4. SPAN stands for Switched Port _______ on Cisco switches.
5. A network _______ is a passive device that copies traffic for analysis without affecting the network.
6. Promiscuous _______ on a NIC allows the adapter to capture all frames on the segment, not just those addressed to it.
7. Packet captures are commonly saved in _______ format (the standard capture file format).

## Part 2: Wireshark

8. Wireshark is the most widely used open-source _______ packet analyzer with a graphical interface.
9. Wireshark's three main panes are the packet _______, packet details, and packet bytes.
10. The packet list pane shows a _______ of all captured packets with timestamp, source, destination, and protocol.
11. The packet _______ pane expands each protocol layer showing field-by-field information.
12. Wireshark can _______ captured traffic to reconstruct entire conversations, such as HTTP sessions.
13. The "Follow _______ Stream" feature in Wireshark reassembles and displays an entire TCP conversation.
14. Wireshark uses color _______ to highlight different types of traffic (green for TCP, blue for DNS, etc.).

## Part 3: Capture Filters

15. Capture filters are applied _______ packets are collected, reducing the volume of data captured.
16. Capture filters use _______ (Berkeley Packet Filter) syntax.
17. The capture filter "host 192.168.1.1" captures only traffic to or _______ the specified IP address.
18. The capture filter "port _______" captures only HTTP traffic.
19. The capture filter "net 10.0.0.0/8" captures traffic for an entire _______ range.
20. The capture filter "tcp" captures only _______ protocol traffic, excluding UDP and other protocols.
21. Capture filters use "and", "or", and "_______" operators to combine filter expressions.

## Part 4: Display Filters

22. Display filters are applied _______ packets have been captured, showing only matching packets.
23. Display filters use Wireshark's own filter _______ which is different from BPF capture filter syntax.
24. The display filter "ip.addr == 10.0.0.1" shows packets where the source _______ destination IP is 10.0.0.1.
25. The display filter "tcp.port == _______" shows only HTTPS traffic.
26. The display filter "http.request._______" filters for specific HTTP request methods like GET or POST.
27. The "dns" display filter shows only _______ Name System query and response packets.
28. Display filters support comparison operators like == (equals), != (not equal), > (greater than), and _______ (contains).

## Part 5: tcpdump and Practical Analysis

29. tcpdump is a _______ -line packet analyzer available on Linux, macOS, and Unix systems.
30. The tcpdump flag "-i" specifies which network _______ to capture traffic on.
31. The tcpdump flag "-w" writes captured packets to a _______ for later analysis.
32. The tcpdump flag "-r" _______ packets from a previously saved capture file.
33. tcpdump uses the same _______ syntax as Wireshark capture filters for filtering traffic.
34. The tcpdump flag "-c" limits the capture to a specific _______ of packets.
35. When analyzing a capture for troubleshooting, look for TCP _______ (RST) packets that indicate abruptly terminated connections.
36. High numbers of TCP _______ indicate packet loss and poor network performance.
37. DNS queries with no _______ packets indicate DNS resolution failures.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: Packet Analysis Fundamentals

1. A packet **analyzer** (also called a protocol analyzer or sniffer) captures and inspects network traffic.
2. Packet analysis operates at the **packet** level, capturing individual frames as they traverse the network.
3. To capture traffic on a switched network, you may need to configure a **SPAN** port (also called mirror port) on the switch.
4. SPAN stands for Switched Port **Analyzer** on Cisco switches.
5. A network **TAP** is a passive device that copies traffic for analysis without affecting the network.
6. Promiscuous **mode** on a NIC allows the adapter to capture all frames on the segment, not just those addressed to it.
7. Packet captures are commonly saved in **PCAP** format (the standard capture file format).

## Part 2: Wireshark

8. Wireshark is the most widely used open-source **GUI** packet analyzer with a graphical interface.
9. Wireshark's three main panes are the packet **list**, packet details, and packet bytes.
10. The packet list pane shows a **summary** of all captured packets with timestamp, source, destination, and protocol.
11. The packet **details** pane expands each protocol layer showing field-by-field information.
12. Wireshark can **reassemble** captured traffic to reconstruct entire conversations, such as HTTP sessions.
13. The "Follow **TCP** Stream" feature in Wireshark reassembles and displays an entire TCP conversation.
14. Wireshark uses color **coding** to highlight different types of traffic (green for TCP, blue for DNS, etc.).

## Part 3: Capture Filters

15. Capture filters are applied **before** packets are collected, reducing the volume of data captured.
16. Capture filters use **BPF** (Berkeley Packet Filter) syntax.
17. The capture filter "host 192.168.1.1" captures only traffic to or **from** the specified IP address.
18. The capture filter "port **80**" captures only HTTP traffic.
19. The capture filter "net 10.0.0.0/8" captures traffic for an entire **network** range.
20. The capture filter "tcp" captures only **TCP** protocol traffic, excluding UDP and other protocols.
21. Capture filters use "and", "or", and "**not**" operators to combine filter expressions.

## Part 4: Display Filters

22. Display filters are applied **after** packets have been captured, showing only matching packets.
23. Display filters use Wireshark's own filter **syntax** which is different from BPF capture filter syntax.
24. The display filter "ip.addr == 10.0.0.1" shows packets where the source **or** destination IP is 10.0.0.1.
25. The display filter "tcp.port == **443**" shows only HTTPS traffic.
26. The display filter "http.request.**method**" filters for specific HTTP request methods like GET or POST.
27. The "dns" display filter shows only **Domain** Name System query and response packets.
28. Display filters support comparison operators like == (equals), != (not equal), > (greater than), and **contains** (contains).

## Part 5: tcpdump and Practical Analysis

29. tcpdump is a **command** -line packet analyzer available on Linux, macOS, and Unix systems.
30. The tcpdump flag "-i" specifies which network **interface** to capture traffic on.
31. The tcpdump flag "-w" writes captured packets to a **file** for later analysis.
32. The tcpdump flag "-r" **reads** packets from a previously saved capture file.
33. tcpdump uses the same **BPF** syntax as Wireshark capture filters for filtering traffic.
34. The tcpdump flag "-c" limits the capture to a specific **count** of packets.
35. When analyzing a capture for troubleshooting, look for TCP **reset** (RST) packets that indicate abruptly terminated connections.
36. High numbers of TCP **retransmissions** indicate packet loss and poor network performance.
37. DNS queries with no **response** packets indicate DNS resolution failures.
hints: A packet analyzer (sniffer) captures and inspects traffic; SPAN ports and TAPs provide access on switched networks.
Promiscuous mode lets a NIC capture all frames on the segment; captures save in PCAP format.
Wireshark has three panes: packet list (summary), packet details (protocol layers), packet bytes (raw hex).
Follow TCP Stream reassembles entire conversations; color coding highlights different traffic types.
Capture filters use BPF syntax and are applied BEFORE capture; display filters apply AFTER capture.
tcpdump is a command-line analyzer; -i = interface, -w = write file, -r = read file, -c = count limit.
tcpdump uses the same BPF syntax as Wireshark capture filters for consistent filtering.
Look for TCP RST (reset) packets for terminated connections, retransmissions for packet loss, missing DNS responses for failures.
